words.definition: the place you are travelling to
example_sentences: Paris is one of the world's most popular tourist destinations.
Our final destination is Rome — we arrive on Thursday.
She always researches her destination carefully before travelling.
words.pronunciation_tip: des-tih-NAY-shun — four syllables, stress on third.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the place I'm going to' — say 'my destination'.

words.definition: the act of leaving a place, especially to start a journey
example_sentences: The departure was delayed by two hours because of technical issues.
Please check the departure board for your gate number.
What time is your departure tomorrow morning?
words.pronunciation_tip: dih-PAR-cher — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the leaving time' — say 'the departure time'.

words.definition: a detailed plan or schedule of places to visit on a trip
example_sentences: She planned an extremely detailed itinerary for the holiday.
Can you send me the itinerary for the conference please?
We had a packed itinerary with activities every single day.
words.pronunciation_tip: eye-TIN-er-air-ee — five syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'programme of travel' — say 'itinerary'.

words.definition: to change from one form of transport to another
example_sentences: We had a two-hour transfer between our connecting flights.
I need to transfer to the red line at the next station.
The transfer from the airport to the hotel takes about 30 minutes.
words.pronunciation_tip: TRANS-fer (noun) / trans-FUR (verb) — stress shifts between noun and verb.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I change trains' in formal contexts — say 'I transfer' or 'make a connection'.

words.definition: a stop during a journey, especially between two flights
example_sentences: We had a four-hour layover in Istanbul airport.
The layover gave us just enough time to explore the terminal.
A long layover can be a great opportunity to see a new city.
words.pronunciation_tip: LAY-oh-ver — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I stop in Istanbul' — say 'I have a layover in Istanbul'.

words.definition: the place at an airport where officials check bags and travel documents
example_sentences: We waited a very long time at customs on the way in.
I had to declare several items at customs on arrival.
Going through customs is always slow during peak travel periods.
words.pronunciation_tip: KUS-tumz — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'immigration' for the bag check — customs checks goods; immigration checks passports.

words.definition: a famous building or place that is very easy to recognise
example_sentences: The Eiffel Tower is Paris's most famous and recognisable landmark.
We visited all the major landmarks of the ancient city.
Use the church tower as a landmark — turn left when you see it.
words.pronunciation_tip: LAND-mark — stress on first syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'famous building' every time — say 'landmark'.

words.definition: a place to stay, such as a hotel, hostel, or holiday apartment
example_sentences: Have you booked your accommodation for the trip yet?
The accommodation was extremely comfortable and very clean.
We're looking for affordable accommodation close to the city centre.
words.pronunciation_tip: uh-kom-uh-DAY-shun — five syllables, stress on fourth.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'hotel' every time — 'accommodation' is more general.

words.definition: having beautiful natural landscapes or attractive views
example_sentences: We deliberately took the longer scenic route along the coast.
The train journey offers breathtaking scenic views of the mountains.
It was a wonderfully scenic drive through the rolling vineyards.
words.pronunciation_tip: SEE-nik — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'beautiful nature view' — say 'scenic view' or 'scenic route'.

words.definition: a small object bought to remember a place you visited
example_sentences: She always buys a small souvenir from every country she visits.
I picked up a few souvenirs at the old city market.
A fridge magnet is the classic tourist souvenir.
words.pronunciation_tip: soo-vuh-NEER — three syllables, stress on last syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'present from the trip' — say 'souvenir'. It specifically means a reminder object.

words.definition: a large bag carried on your back, or to travel cheaply with one
example_sentences: She travelled across Europe with just one small backpack.
He backpacked through Southeast Asia for six whole months.
A backpacker's hostel is much cheaper than a proper hotel.
words.pronunciation_tip: BAK-pak — stress on first syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'rucksack travel' — say 'backpacking' for budget travel style.

words.definition: extreme tiredness caused by travelling across several time zones
example_sentences: I always get terrible jet lag after flying to Asia or America.
It took me a full week to recover from the jet lag.
Coffee and willpower don't cure jet lag — only time does.
words.pronunciation_tip: JET-lag — two words, equal stress.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I'm tired because of the time difference' — say 'I have jet lag'.
words.collocations: ['suffer from jet lag', 'recover from jet lag', 'bad jet lag']

words.definition: to plan and find the correct route to a destination
example_sentences: She navigated the winding mountain roads perfectly.
I used my phone to navigate through the unfamiliar city.
It's very hard to navigate without any kind of map.
words.pronunciation_tip: NAV-ih-gayt — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I found the way' formally — say 'I navigated' or 'I used GPS to navigate'.

words.definition: having sold more reservations than there are available spaces
example_sentences: Our flight was overbooked and two passengers were bumped.
The hotel was completely overbooked during the music festival.
We were offered compensation because our flight was overbooked.
words.pronunciation_tip: oh-ver-BOOKT — stress on second syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'they sold too many tickets' — say 'the flight was overbooked'.

words.definition: a long, physically challenging walk usually in nature
example_sentences: We went on a memorable two-day trek through the national park.
It was a very tough trek to reach the mountain summit.
She trekked all the way across the Sahara Desert.
words.pronunciation_tip: TREK — one syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'a long walk' only — 'trek' implies something challenging in nature.

words.definition: the system of money used in a particular country
example_sentences: What currency do they use in Japan?
I need to exchange some currency before I leave for the trip.
The local currency is the Thai baht.
words.pronunciation_tip: KUR-un-see — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'money of the country' — say 'local currency'.

words.definition: in a remote or less visited location away from tourists
example_sentences: We discovered a wonderful village completely off the beaten track.
She always prefers to explore places off the beaten track.
The best local restaurants are often found off the beaten track.
words.pronunciation_tip: off the BEAT-un track.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'in a hidden place' — say 'off the beaten track'.
words.collocations: ['completely off the beaten track', 'find somewhere off the beaten track', 'travel off the beaten track']

words.definition: confusion or difficulty experienced when encountering a very different culture
example_sentences: She experienced real culture shock when she moved to Japan.
Culture shock can take several months to properly overcome.
The food, language, and customs all contributed to his culture shock.
words.pronunciation_tip: KUL-cher SHOK.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I was confused by the different culture' — say 'I experienced culture shock'.
words.collocations: ['experience culture shock', 'overcome culture shock', 'culture shock can be']

words.definition: the activity of visiting famous or interesting places as a tourist
example_sentences: We spent the first day sightseeing around the historic old city.
Are you interested in doing some sightseeing while you're here?
There is so much incredible sightseeing to do in Rome.
words.pronunciation_tip: SYT-see-ing — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'visiting tourist places' — say 'sightseeing'.

words.definition: a holiday where flights and accommodation are included in one price
example_sentences: We booked a cheap package holiday to Turkey last minute.
Package holidays are often significantly cheaper than booking separately.
She prefers independent travel to pre-arranged package holidays.
words.pronunciation_tip: PAK-ij HOL-ih-day.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'all-in-one holiday' — say 'package holiday'.
words.collocations: ['book a package holiday', 'cheap package holiday', 'go on a package holiday']
